items.title: ‘Our bodies contain poison’: Landmark trial in Argentina probes dangers of agrochemicals
items.description: Trial over agrochemical spraying in the city of Pergamino has set an unprecedented legal benchmark: for the first time, a federal court placed the entire agricultural production chain in the dock, from farmers and agronomists to public officials. items.title: From colonial symbol to sanctuary: Paris's Grand Mosque turns 100
items.description: The Grand Mosque of Paris, built in 1926 in the Latin Quarter as a tribute to Muslim soldiers who died for France during World War I, marks its 100th anniversary this year. French historian Benjamin Stora traces its little-known role as a geopolitical bridge – not only between France and Algeria, but also between Muslims and Jews.

He also released documents he says reveal foreign interference, despite intelligence officials saying there is no evidence that foreign actors changed the outcome of a US election.

items.title: Ukrainians 'disillusioned, outraged': Zelensky 'reshuffled entire govt' to dismiss defence minister
items.description: François Picard is pleased to welcome Daria Kaleniuk, Executive Director of the Anti-Corruption Action Center. Ukraine's latest defense leadership shake-up, as interpreted by Kaleniuk, is not as a routine personnel change but rather a struggle over the future direction of wartime governance. Her analysis frames the dismissal of the reformist defense minister as a contest between competing institutional logics: one prioritising transparency, competitive procurement, civilian oversight, and data-driven reform, and another associated with entrenched networks, military hierarchy, and resistance to institutional change.

items.title: Incoming British prime minister Andy Burnham hopes to begin 'an era of hope'
items.description: The Labour party in the UK has a new leader. Andy Burnham is succeeding to outgoing prime minister Keir Starmer, and will be officially confirmed as prime minister on July 20. In his speech to Labour members at a special conference, he vowed to bring hope back to the UK. He is seen as the party's main hope to reverse the poll and take back votes from Reform UK. FRANCE 24's Benedicte Paviot tells us more from London.

'The King of the North' was elected in a snap election by a landslide in Makerfield, a constituency near Manchester. He will become the UK's seventh prime minister in a decade, with Labour MPs betting Burnham is the party's best chance of reining in Nigel Farage's anti-immigrant Reform UK party. items.title: 'Not a big deal': Amid protests, Venice's new mayor welcomes US ambassador's 117m superyacht visit
items.description: François Picard is pleased to welcome Venice's new mayor, Simone Venturini, just one month into office, He offers a unique governing philosophy rooted in balancing contradictions rather than embracing absolutes. His central argument is that Venice cannot be understood merely as an open-air museum threatened by mass tourism, nor simply as a global cultural icon. Instead, he portrays it as a living, breathing city that must simultaneously preserve its fragile heritage, remain open to international exchange, and reinvent its economic future.

items.title: What challenges await the UK's incoming Prime Minister Andy Burnham?
items.description: The UK will be getting a new prime minister on July 20. And many challenges await Andy Burnham. He will be faced with a tepid economy, high government borrowing costs, and irregular migrants arriving in small boats that have fuelled support for Reform. In his address to the Labour party, he has vowed to boost the construction of public housing to try to resolve the homelessness crisis, and pump resources into social care. FRANCE 24's Rochelle Ferguson-Bouyahi looks at what awaits the incoming UK prime minister.

items.title: Milei planning trips to Brazil, Peru, Ecuador, Colombia – and UK?
items.description: Argentina’s leader is preparing for a fresh round of regional diplomacy, including a visit to see former Brazilian president Jair Bolsonaro, a trip to Peru for the inauguration of Keiko Fujimori, and bilateral meetings with Ecuador's Daniel Noboa and Colombia's president-elect Abelardo de la Espriella. Oh, and then there’s the issue of a trip to London… Leer más

items.title: Cities are getting smarter about water and other eco wins
items.description: From flood-resistant infrastructure to new ways of securing drinking water, cities are adapting to the growing climate pressures of a hotter, drier world. Plus, tourists pitching in to help sea turtles.

items.title: Who are Russia's 3,000 African army recruits fighting in Ukraine?
items.description: Russia continues to recruit Africans to fight on the front in Ukraine. The Stop Russian Recruiters platform, which is linked to Urkaine's Ministry of Defence, has now identified nearly 3,000 recruits. Around one in six has already been killed. Among the countries most affected are Cameroon, Ghana, Egypt and Kenya, where FRANCE 24's Bastien Renouil tells us more.

items.title: US slaps 25% tariffs on Brazil with election looming
items.description: The US will impose a 25% tariff on most imports from Brazil starting later this month. It's the first action in the Trump administration's new tariff strategy, after the Supreme Court struck down its prior impositions.

items.title: Controversial Sunshine Coast housing development fast-tracked
items.description: The Queensland government declares the Halls Creek development a priority development area. The project is expected to deliver 12,000 new homes.

items.title: More than 500 Rohingya vanished at sea
items.description: Two boats carrying an estimated 530 Rohingyas have disappeared since leaving Myanmar on 29 June.

items.title: Rubio says United States wants to globalise fight against far-left 'terrorism'
items.description: Argentina among nations represented at meeting on "The Resurgence of Political Terrorism" hosted by US Secretary of State Marco Rubio. Leer más

items.title: Trump accuses China of interfering in US elections ahead of midterms
items.description: In an address to the nation, he made unverified claims that Beijing interfered in the 2020 polls which he lost.

items.title: U.S. airstrikes on Iran appear to have damaged Chabahar port
items.description: Chabahar port has been a repeated target of American airstrikes. Iranian state media acknowledged a third round of strikes on the facility without immediately acknowledging the tower's collapse

items.title: Moira Deeming ousted as Liberal candidate after apology stand-off
items.description: Moira Deeming's political career as a Liberal is over after the party's Victorian state executive voted to revoke the MP's candidacy for the November state election.

items.title: West Asia war updates: U.S. launches new strikes on Iran; explosions heard in south
items.description: U.S. Vice ‌President J.D. Vance says some members of the Israeli government tried to influence U.S. public opinion to oppose a deal by the U.S. to end the ⁠war with Iran

items.title: Mongolian throat singing goes global as ancient art finds new fans
items.description: Mongolian throat singing is gaining new audiences worldwide, as artists like Batzorig Vaanchig blend the ancient nomadic tradition of khoomei with modern forms of entertainment, from cinema to video games.

items.title: As Milei’s aura fades, Argentina starts to look for a third way
items.description: With President Milei’s popularity at its lowest level in more than two years, talk of the 2027 election has taken over investor trips and political circles. The question being asked is whether it might be the moment for a third way. Leer más

items.title: Kyiv hit again as Ukraine reports two dead in Russian strike
items.description: Russian missiles killed two people and wounded five, including a child, in Kyiv, Ukraine, on 16 July 2026.

items.title: What is Greater Tunb Island, the strategic point in the Strait of Hormuz targeted by U.S. airstrikes?
items.description: Greater Tunb is a small island in the Persian Gulf near the entrance to the Strait of Hormuz and is regarded as a strategically important location in the strait

items.title: Two Nigerien journalists freed after months in detention
items.description: Two journalists in Niger have been released from prison, their families and a court source confirmed Wednesday.

items.title: I wouldn't marry him until he paid off his debt, now I'm in charge of our money
items.description: Sarah and her husband have shared one account for 25 years, but she says managing it falls to her.

items.title: Taco Bell removes lettuce from menu in US after links to explosive diarrhoea
items.description: Lettuce from one supplier is being removed indefinitely "out of an abundance of caution", the company says.

items.title: Trump's speech a desperate move — and 'ominous sign' ahead of midterms
items.description: Using a prime-time address to the nation to repeat long-debunked claims about the 2020 election defeat is a desperate and unusual move. Analysts say it's an "ominous sign" of how Donald Trump may handle the upcoming midterms.

items.title: NSW woman arrested as part of Dezi Freeman investigation
items.description: A NSW woman was arrested in Bairnsdale today as part of Victoria Police's investigation into the movements of dead police killer Dezi Freeman.

items.title: Ukraine says it destroyed Russian bomber at Engels airbase
items.description: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy said the military plane was used by Russia for missile strikes on Ukraine.

items.title: Bahrain and Kuwait face Iranian fire as U.S. airstrikes expand to target more bridges
items.description: For the first time in this latest round of violence, strikes also reached into areas around Iran's capital, Tehran, showing a widening set of targets for the Americans

items.title: Toyota eyes lunar rover powered by regenerative fuel-cell tech
items.description: Toyota has teamed up with JAXA since 2019 to develop the manned lunar rover — which it dubbed the Lunar Cruiser — that they hope can be put on the moon in 2029.

items.title: New approach for survivors of sexual violence
items.description: In this edition: how Belgium is leading the way in treating survivors of sexual violence by offering a one-stop centre which provides them with access to medical, psychological and police support. Also, as South Korea is wrestling with a fertility crisis a group of monks at a local monastery are doing their bit to revive romance among the young. Plus, we meet Yoa, a rising star of French music who has turned personal pain into powerful songs – and gained many fans in the process. *The 51 Percent will be taking a break over the European summer and will return in early September with a brand-new season.

items.title: 150 years of the Bayreuth Festival: Confronting a troubled legacy
items.description: Should the past be confronted — or avoided? Why the Wagner festival's handling of its Nazi-era legacy remains a contentious issue in its 150th anniversary year.

items.title: I thought we'd grow old together, says band leader whose girlfriend died in Thai bar inferno
items.description: Athipat Wichan tells BBC Thai he regrets bringing his girlfriend Nahathai into his indie band Thotsakan.

items.title: Extreme heat and weather conditions attributed to stagnant jet stream
items.description: It’s no coincidence that extreme heat is engulfing huge swaths of Asia, Europe and North America all at the same time.

items.title: Fact check: Trump revives US election fraud claims
items.description: In a primetime address, US President Donald Trump alleged Chinese voter interference in the 2020 US election. DW examined the declassified documents behind his claims.

items.title: Netflix tumbles as slowing growth, less viewership data spook investors
How the new U.S. visa rules affect your online privacy | Explained
items.description: Privacy experts have slammed the U.S. government’s new social media vetting requirements, likening the country’s approach to that of an authoritarian regime. However, they note it is difficult to claim that privacy laws are being broken as visa applicants can choose to ignore the request, though they put their visa application at risk by doing so

items.title: EQT raises Kakaku.com offer price to 3,450 yen, topping rival bid
'Works every time': Superstition ramps up ahead in Argentina of World Cup final
items.description: Football fans are putting their faith in good luck rituals ahead of Sunday's World Cup final. But these wacky habits are no joke to those who practice them. Leer más

items.title: One person reportedly killed as Algeria battles wildfires
items.description: Wildfires burning across northern Algeria have killed a municipal worker who was taking part in firefighting, media reports said Wednesday.

items.title: Women sand miners toil stripped Cape Verde beach
items.description: On a rocky Cape Verde beach, Maria and Vania fill their buckets with black sand the moment that waves recede, transporting it on their heads to a stockpile where it will be sold illegally.

items.title: Centuries-old hunt beckons the rain in western Senegal
items.description: In western Senegal, armed men on motorcycles took part in the 723rd edition of the "Miss de Diobaye” hunt, a centuries-old tradition believing to bring rain and protect the community.

items.title: Pix of the Day, 17 July 2026
items.description: Pix of the Day, 17 July 2026

items.title: Japan's ruling party warns about suspected collusion between activist investors and private equity
Argentina inflation slows to lowest level since last August
items.description: Inflation slowed for a third straight month to the lowest level since August in a victory for President Javier Milei. Leer más

items.title: Merz and Macron: A Franco-German week of harmony
items.description: Friedrich Merz and Emmanuel Macron did everything they could this week to demonstrate a close partnership. This is likely because both fear that the next French president could be nationalist Marine Le Pen.

items.title: UN urges probe into deaths in Pakistani-administered Kashmir unrest
items.description: Kashmir clashes have killed 31 since June, leading UN rights chief Volker Turk to appeal for calm.

items.title: At least 20 children and one adult were killed in a school bus crash in Uganda, police say
items.description: The bus belonged to the King David Junior School, an elementary school situated in the capital Kampala

items.title: Nude shopper admits fourth indecent act in Mount Gambier
items.description: A Mount Gambier man who previously admitted going naked in two local food businesses pleads guilty to another charge of indecent behaviour, as his case heads to the new South Australian Veterans Court.

items.title: German Chancellor Friedrich Merz makes appeal to AfD voters
items.description: Germany's chancellor has insisted that his coalition has "regained its footing" — despite dismal poll numbers. Friedrich Merz also cautioned against voting for the far-right AfD party.

items.title: What if Bluey spoke one of the world's oldest living languages?
items.description: Five episodes of the award-winning children’s cartoon have been released in Australian Indigenous language for the first time.

items.title: German tenants need better heat protection
items.description: Heat waves are becoming longer, hotter and more frequent. Record temperatures have left many tenants struggling. Now Chancellor Friedrich Merz has said the building law needs to be amended.

items.title: Ten years ago, parts of the Turkish military tried to overthrow the Erdogan government. The consequences are still shaping Turkey today
items.description: Ten years ago, parts of the Turkish military attempted to overthrow the government of President Recep Tayyip Erdogan. The consequences of the failed coup continue to shape Turkey to this day. DW looks back.

items.title: Israel tells Pentagon chief will keep troops in Lebanon, Syria, Gaza 'security zones'
items.description: His comments come days after U.S. President Donald Trump asked Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to pull Israeli forces out of Syria and Lebanon

items.title: Spain battles major Aragon wildfire amid heatwave
items.description: Spain has deployed more than 400 firefighters and 19 aircraft to combat a fast-moving wildfire in the northeastern region of Aragon after flames swept across around 7,600 hectares by 16 July.

items.title: No support for Jews fleeing Nazi Germany at Evian conference
items.description: In July 1938, a conference was held in Evian, France, to address the issue of Jews being persecuted by the Nazis in Germany and Austria. Almost nobody in the world was willing to take them in as refugees.

items.title: Cameroon launches tax recovery operation in the gold sector
items.description: This is part of a series of new measures to regain control over the gold sector to enhance its contribution to economic development.

items.title: South African police probe murder of anti-immigrant provincial leader
items.description: The acting police chief has set up a multidisciplinary team to investigate the killing of March and March's Andile Mvuyiselwa Somgaxa.

items.title: Ivory Coast fashion designers storm the international stage
items.description: Favoured by the likes of Beyoncé and Aya Nakamura, Ivory Coast fashion is wooing the world stage, thanks in part to showcasing on social media.

items.title: China’s Xi Jinping launches new AI alliance: What is it?
items.description: Beijing is likely to use the WAICO alliance to influence global AI regulations, analysts say.

items.title: What to know about the Canadian and US wildfires and their impact
items.description: Wildfires in Ontario have blanketed US and Canadian cities with dangerous smoke.

items.title: Lawyers clash in Maradona death trial, forcing hearing to halt
items.description: Judges suspend proceedings and warn they could remove counsel from the case after a heated confrontation between rival lawyers nearly turns physical. Leer más

items.title: Philippines condemns monkey video on Chinese media as racist
items.description: The AI video on China Daily showed a monkey in Philippine garments singing its claims to the South China Sea.

items.title: Iran accuses the United States of bombing civilian infrastructure
items.description: Iranian state media are reporting that bridges, a train station, and an airport were among the sites hit, amid renewed airstrikes.

items.title: Dollar steadies on safe-haven demand but set for weekly decline
